background.usage: Light theme background and card surface.

foreground.usage: Primary text, primary button background, active emphasis, and compact brand mark.

surface-muted.usage: Muted fills, secondary controls, docs table headers, toolbar backgrounds.

text-muted.usage: Descriptions, support copy, secondary navigation text.

border.usage: One-pixel borders, dividers, inputs, tables, and outlines.

accent-forge.usage: Focus rings, check icons, selected badges, security signals, and subtle terminal glow.

destructive.usage: Blocking and failure states, always paired with text.

display.purpose: Hero headlines and first-viewport positioning only.

headline.purpose: Section headings and docs page titles.

body.purpose: Marketing body copy and documentation prose.

label.purpose: Navigation, buttons, badges, controls, and compact UI labels.

code.purpose: Commands, JSON, package coordinates, hashes, and terminal output.

anvilAmbient.usage: Hero imagery, code panels, and important media-like surfaces.

default.usage: Buttons, nav links, badges, and hover states.

button.notes: Rounded 6px controls with 40px default height, focus-visible 2px ring, and lucide icon support.

card.notes: 8px radius, one-pixel border, card background, 24px padding, shadow-sm.

badge.notes: Compact 6px radius badges with semibold 12px text and optional semantic state color.

siteHeader.notes: Sticky 64px header with border, translucent background, backdrop blur, desktop nav, and mobile docs icon.

codePanel.notes: Signature terminal component with toolbar, copy action, JetBrains Mono content, and code-window gradient.

dos: Lead with concrete commands, policy decisions, install flows, analysis artefacts, and audit trails.
Keep Forge Orange rare and meaningful.
Use visible one-pixel borders for structure.
Pair security states with text, iconography, and color.
Use JetBrains Mono only for actual code and command content.
donts: Do not make Anvil feel like glossy cybersecurity vendor sludge.
Do not use generic SaaS dashboard wallpaper, hacker-terminal cosplay, or corporate DevSecOps content mulch.
Do not use inflated claims, fake urgency, black-and-neon threat theatre, vague compliance language, or heroic metrics with no receipts.
Do not use colored side-stripe borders, gradient text, decorative glassmorphism, or identical card grids as the main design answer.
Do not let color alone communicate security states.
